column32: You are going to be getting a lot of tea, I'm afraid. So, my parents are now divorced but growing up still would be serious fights going on in our household and my siblings and I would basically be doing damage control, moderating and solving the fight altogether. Think of a cricket match between my parents and 13 year old me being the referee everytime- that best describes the dynamic. This was my point of conflict that I usually faced with my parents. Oh yeah also my father is narcissistic and has bipolar disorder (both diagnosed by a clinical psychologist so I'm not over-extraggerating or pathologizing) so growing up with him in general was a challenge but our interactions were also limited since he was in army and was living in the areas he was posted at. To help you make sense of the timeline,  I was 12 when he retired from army, 15 when he divorced my mom and I'm currently 20.

column31: In the beginning, my relationship with both of my parents was positive, but after I received low grades in one of my board exams, my relationship with my father changed. Since then, he often reminds me of that experience and focuses mainly on my studies whenever we talk. Because of this, I no longer feel comfortable sharing personal things with him and tend to avoid spending time together at home.
column32: One common point of conflict I faced with my parents was around balancing my studies and my mental well-being. As a 19-year-old dealing with academic pressure, I sometimes wanted to go out to relax, but my parents felt I should prioritize my studies instead. This created tension, as I had not been used to going out alone before, but with time I began to feel the need for it.

column31:
Here's a possible reflection:

One aspect of my relationship with my parents that I valued the most was the stability and security they provided through financial support and a home. Having my basic needs met allowed me to focus on my personal growth, education, and goals.
column32: One common challenge I faced with my parents was feeling like they didn't fully understand me, which often led to conflicts. As the eldest, I took on a lot of responsibility for taking care of my siblings, which added to the tension. I sometimes felt like I was shouldering the burden of parenting my siblings while my parents were less involved, leading to frustration and misunderstandings.
